coredata.dc:title: Algorithm-driven optimization of lithium-ion battery thermal modeling
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Sun, Zeyu
Guo, Yue
Zhang, Cheng
Zhou, Quan
Xu, Hongming
Wang, Chongming
coredata.dc:description: Detailed modeling of battery thermal behaviour has high computational demand due to the presence of multi-scale and multi-physics phenomena. For battery module/pack level simulation, a simple and accurate battery heat generation estimation is urgently required. This paper investigates the optimization of thermal numerical modeling for cylindrical 21,700 lithium-ion batteries with a nominal capacity of 5 Ah. A 3-dimensional battery model was built in the Multiphysics simulation software, COMSOL. The heat source of the model adopts a commonly used heat generation model incorporating irreversible and reversible heat. Correction factors, as a function of the state of charge, were introduced to the calculation of irreversible heat item. The Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO) algorithm, written in MATLAB, was coupled with the COMSOL numerical model to minimize the prediction error by varying correction factors. Battery surface temperature data under the continuous discharge tests (0.5C–3.5C) and dynamic loads were experimentally obtained and used to validate the model. The simulation results of the unoptimized model showed a discrepancy of up to 5 °C with the experimental data. After optimization, the prediction error was reduced to less than 0.5 °C on average. The optimized model was applied to predict the thermal behaviour of a battery module (16 aged cells) using oil-based immersion cooling. The pristine battery module with coolant flow velocities of 0.01 m/s was chosen as the baseline. The results indicate that the aged battery modules with internal resistance of 50 mΩ and 75 mΩ require coolant flow velocities of 0.05 m/s and 0.12 m/s, respectively, to achieve the baseline temperature. The study highlights a high-precision and low-computational cost approach for heat generation calculation of lithium-ion batteries is provided, which contributes to the development of battery thermal management systems.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lithium-ion battery
Thermal modeling
Particle Swarm Optimization
Thermal management
Immersion cooling

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Constant current and dynamic load test
Calibration and validation for heat generation model
Temperature rising of the battery module

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Test facilities: (a) MTI Spot welder; (b) Battery cell; (c) Binder thermal chamber; (d) Bio-logic cycler.
Current profile of a dynamic load test.
(a) Example of the voltage variation during the thermal cycle for SOC 20 %; (b) dU/dT vs. SOC.
Schematic of the battery module model.
Optimization flow chat based on Co-simulation platform.
Comparison of results from simulation and experiments: (a, c) before optimization; (b, d) after optimization.
Validation of the thermal module: (a, c) before optimization; (b, d) after optimization.
Temperature evolution of the battery module at various coolant velocities under the current rate of: (a) 1C; (b) 2C; (c) 3C.
Average temperature of battery module at various coolant velocities under the current rate of: (a) 1C; (b) 2C; (c) 3C.
Temperature evolution of selected cells at various coolant velocities.

Maximum cell-to-cell temperature difference in the module using various flow velocities at 3C discharge: (a) T
threshold = 35 °C; (b) T
threshold = 60 °C.
Thermophysical properties of the cell and mineral oil.
Pressure drop and power consumption of oil cooling.

head.ce:title: Algorithm-driven optimization of lithium-ion battery thermal modeling

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institutes for Future Transport and Cities, Coventry University, Coventry CV1 5FB, United Kingdom

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institutes for Future Transport and Cities

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institutes for Future Transport and Cities, Coventry University, Coventry, United Kingdom, CV1 5FB

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Mechanical Engineering, University of Birmingham, Birmingham B15 2TT, United Kingdom
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Mechanical Engineering, University of Birmingham, Birmingham, United Kingdom, B15 2TT

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Detailed modeling of battery thermal behaviour has high computational demand due to the presence of multi-scale and multi-physics phenomena. For battery module/pack level simulation, a simple and accurate battery heat generation estimation is urgently required. This paper investigates the optimization of thermal numerical modeling for cylindrical 21,700 lithium-ion batteries with a nominal capacity of 5 Ah. A 3-dimensional battery model was built in the Multiphysics simulation software, COMSOL. The heat source of the model adopts a commonly used heat generation model incorporating irreversible and reversible heat. Correction factors, as a function of the state of charge, were introduced to the calculation of irreversible heat item. The Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO) algorithm, written in MATLAB, was coupled with the COMSOL numerical model to minimize the prediction error by varying correction factors. Battery surface temperature data under the continuous discharge tests (0.5C–3.5C) and dynamic loads were experimentally obtained and used to validate the model. The simulation results of the unoptimized model showed a discrepancy of up to 5 °C with the experimental data. After optimization, the prediction error was reduced to less than 0.5 °C on average. The optimized model was applied to predict the thermal behaviour of a battery module (16 aged cells) using oil-based immersion cooling. The pristine battery module with coolant flow velocities of 0.01 m/s was chosen as the baseline. The results indicate that the aged battery modules with internal resistance of 50 mΩ and 75 mΩ require coolant flow velocities of 0.05 m/s and 0.12 m/s, respectively, to achieve the baseline temperature. The study highlights a high-precision and low-computational cost approach for heat generation calculation of lithium-ion batteries is provided, which contributes to the development of battery thermal management systems.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Lithium-ion batteries (LiBs) are widely used in electric vehicles for reducing air pollution and addressing global warming [1]. LiBs generate heat, especially during high-power applications [2]. If the heat generated by LiBs is not effectively dissipated, it will continuously accumulate heat and induce thermal hazards, such as cell swelling and accelerated degradation [3,4]. In the worst scenarios, this can lead to thermal runaways, fires, and even explosions [5]. The optimal operating temperature for LiBs is in the range of 15 °C–40 °C, and the temperature gradient in a battery pack should be controlled below 5 °C to ensure the degradation rate uniformity among cells [6]. High-temperature conditions accelerate battery degradation and reduce the lifetime of LiBs [7]. To maintain performance and extend the cycle life of LiBs, various battery thermal management systems (BTMs) have been developed, such as air-based cooling [8], liquid-based cooling [9], phase change materials cooling [10], and heat pipe cooling [11].

Conventionally, electric vehicle manufacturers adopt indirect liquid cooling technology based on the cold plate or a serpentine flow channel. The heat generated by the battery is transferred to heat transfer fluid (HTF) through metallic heat exchangers. The surface of a metal heat exchanger is typically coated with a dielectric coating with low thermal conductivity, which causes high thermal resistance. Higher thermal resistance or a limited contact area both reduce the heat dissipation performance of BTMs and the temperature gradient. Deng et al. [21] proposed oil-based immersion cooling for battery modules. The results illustrated that the heat transfer of oil with higher viscosity is 1.5–3 times that of air cooling at the same pressure drop. Immersion cooling can significantly reduce the complexity of BTMs [12,13]. Pulugundla et al. [14] experimentally and numerically investigate the thermal behaviour of lithium-nickel-cobalt-aluminum oxide (NCA) LiB cells with immersion cooling. Patil et al. [15] used a CFD model for investigating the dielectric immersion cooling of a battery pack consisting of 14 pouch cells at a 3C discharge rate. The thermal behaviour of cylindrical battery module level with immersion cooling has not been investigated fully. Computational fluid dynamics (CFD) simulation is an effective method to predict the thermal behaviour of LiBs based on spatial and transient solutions, which are commonly used to assist the development of BTMs [16,17]. Karimi et al. [18] established a 2D CFD model to evaluate the influence of cooling conditions and stack configuration on the temperature distribution of the battery module. Tian et al. [19] conducted heat transmission of LiB using CFD techniques and quantized the economic cost of air and phase change materials thermal management solution. Mesgarpour et al. [20] proposed a novel approach that combined pattern-based artificial neural networks and CFD numerical simulation to predict the thermal response of the battery and significantly reduced calculation time.

Accurately calculating battery heat generation is a challenge in conducting thermal modeling. Sun et al. [21] established an electrochemical-thermal model to predict the heat generation rate of a 945 mAh lithium titanate battery during charging and discharging processes. Several researchers employed electrochemical models to calculate the heat source of battery cells. Dong et al. [22] investigated the effect of entropy change on heat generation by an electrochemical model. Gomadam et al. [23] established an electrochemical-thermal coupled model to simulate the temperature profile of a battery cell in spiral geometries. Chen et al. [24] adopted a one-dimensional electrochemical model to calculate the heat generation of LiBs. However, a detailed numerical simulation based on electrochemistry is highly complex due to the presence of multi-scale and multi-physics phenomena [25]. Kim et al. [26] proposed a cooperative optimization method combining the Genetic algorithm (GA) and neural network for the battery electrochemical model. Rahman et al. [27] identified the electrochemical model parameter based on the particle swarm optimization (PSO) algorithm. Compared to other metaheuristic algorithms, the PSO algorithm has fewer tuning parameters [28]. To simplify the complicated electrochemical model, the general energy balance model proposed by Bernardi et al. [29] divided battery generation into reversible heat and irreversible heat. Inui et al. [30] employed a simplified internal equivalent circuit to calculate the heat generation of lithium iron phosphate. Yuksel et al. [31] developed a battery degradation model and studied the influence of BTMs on battery life. Compared with batteries without a thermal management system, the active air-based BTMS can double battery life. Prahit et al. [32] conducted a comparative study for different liquid-based BTMs, and the heat generation of batteries is calculated by equivalent internal resistance based on Ohm's law.

This research aims to develop a high-precision and low-computational cost battery thermal dynamic model, which will aid in guiding the design of BTMs. The novelty of this study is that a heat generation equation for LiB was improved by introducing a set of correction factors into the irreversible heat generation term. Those correction factors were optimized by Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO) algorithm written in MATLAB coupled with a 3-dimensional battery numerical model built in COMSOL Multiphysics. Experiments of 0.5C–3.5C continuous discharge and dynamic load tests were conducted on 5 Ah 21,700 batteries and the results were used to calibrate and validate the model. The optimized thermal model was applied to simulate the thermal behaviour of a small-scale battery module (16 aged cells) using oil-based immersion cooling. Furthermore, the influence of mineral oil flow velocity, the key design parameter of BTMs, on the heat dissipation performance of aged battery modules with different internal resistances was further evaluated. The structure of this paper is organized as follows: Sections 2 and 3 introduce the experimental setup and simulation procedures, respectively. Results and discussion are presented in Section 4. Finally, in Section 5, the key findings are summarized.

Fig. 1 shows relevant test facilities. The battery cells tested in this study are commercial 21,700 LiNi0.8Mn0.1Co0.1O2 (NMC811) batteries with a nominal capacity of 5 Ah. These cells are highly consistent in their energy content (4979.0 ± 15.1 mAh) and internal resistance (R
ohm = 25.22 ± 0.25 mΩ), which gives us confidence in their quality and suitability for further academic study without the concerns of unexpected cell-to-cell performance discrepancy. Nickel tables (22 mm × 8 mm × 0.15 mm) were welded to the battery electrical terminals using an MTI spot welder. Low-resistant brass blocks were bolted at constant torque to clear acrylic sheets with positioning holes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Constant current and dynamic load test
ce:section.ce:para: The batteries were placed in a Binder thermal chamber with a pre-defined temperature, and they were connected to the Bio-logic cycler. LiBs were charged with constant-current constant-voltage (CC-CV) charging (current: 1/3C; upper voltage limit: 4.2 V; cut-off current: 250 mA). After a 2-hour rest, batteries were discharged either by a constant-current (CC) discharge procedure (lower voltage limit: 2.75 V) or by a dynamic load profile. The dynamic load profile is presented in Fig. 2 . The cell temperature was monitored and recorded (sensor: K-type thermocouple; sampling position: middle of the metal can). Five repeats were conducted for each test and error bars were calculated.

Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS) was used to measure battery impedance in Bio-logic. EIS was performed on batteries in frequencies ranging from 10 mHz to 100 kHz (10 points per decade) with a current amplitude of 100 mA (approximately C/50). R
ohm , the sum of the ohmic resistance of binder, current collector, electrodes, and electrolyte, was obtained by identifying the intersection between the impedance spectrum and the real axis in the high-frequency region of Nyquist plots.

Battery cells with a known SOC (0 %–100 % with a 5 % interval) will be placed in the Binder thermal chamber. The thermal chamber was programmed with conduct thermal cycles (0 °C–50 °C), wherein at each temperature, there was a 3-hour rest. The voltage variation during the thermal cycle was recorded. dU/dT was calculated by dividing the voltage difference by temperature variation. Fig. 3 show the voltage variation during thermal cycle for SOC 20% and temperature coefficient under different SOC.

The heat generation of LiBs is mainly composed of four elements [2]: reaction heat value Qr , polarization heat value Qp , Joule heat value Qj , and side reaction heat
Q s
. The total heat generation
Q t
could be expressed as follow:
(1)
Q t
= Qr + Qp + Qj +
Q s

Based on the Bernardi equation [29], the heat generation of LiBs can be simplified as the sum of reversible reaction heat and irreversible heat. The heat generation of a single cell could be written as follow:
(2)
Q irr
=
I 2
R ohm
(3)
Q rev
= − IT
dU dT
(4)
Q t
=
I 2
R ohm
+ IT
dU dT

The internal resistance of the cell causes irreversible heat generation during charging/discharging, while reversible heat is generated by entropy changes in the reaction between the cathode and anode. A modified Eq. (5) was used in a COMSOL single-cell 3D thermal model to reduce the error between the model and experiment data. In the modified equation, f(soc) is multiplied by
Q irr
, and there are eight correction factors in f(soc), which will be optimized to minimize the difference between the simulation and experimental temperature data. The modified heat generation equation of a single cell could be expressed as follow:
(5)
Q t
=
I 2
R ohm
f
soc
+
dU dT
TI
where I is current and it is positive in charging, and negative in discharging;
R ohm
is the internal resistance of the battery cell, and it is SOC-dependent;
dU dT
is a parameter related to the electrochemical reaction, also called the temperature coefficient.
dU dT
is a function of SOC, and it is measured via measuring the steady state voltage of the battery under temperature sweep at various SOC.

According to thermodynamic theory, the heat exchange between LiB cells and the external environment is mainly through thermal conduction, thermal convection, and thermal radiation. To facilitate the numerical simulation analysis of the temperature of the LiB, some assumptions are as follows: 1) Heat radiation between LiB cells and their surroundings has negligible influence on heat dissipation and is therefore ignored in the simulation; 2) The internal materials of the battery are uniform, and the thermophysical properties of each material are not affected by temperature; 3) The generated heat is uniformly distributed inside the battery cell; 4) Mineral oil is considered incompressible Newtonian fluid; 5) The thermophysical properties of mineral oil are constant and isotropic.

The energy conservation equation of the battery cell can be described as follows:
(6)
mC
∂T ∂t
= Q − hA
T b
−
T oil

Based on the assumptions made in the previous section, the three-dimensional differential equation in the cylindrical coordinates can be expressed as follows:
(7)
ρC
∂T ∂t
=
1 r
∂ ∂r
λ r
r
∂T ∂r
+
1
r 2
∂ ∂φ
λ φ
∂ ∂φ
+
∂ ∂z
λ z
∂T ∂z
+
Q ̇

In the domain of direct liquid cooling, fluid flow, and heat transfer are governed by the continuity, momentum conservation, and energy equations:
(8)
∂ρ ∂t
+ ∇
ρ
V ⃑
= 0
(9)
ρ
∂
V ⃑
∂t
+ ρ
V ⃑
∇
V ⃑
= − ∇ p + μ
∇ 2
V ⃑
(10)
∂ ∂t
ρ
C oil
T oil
+ ∇
V ⃑
ρ
C oil
T oil
= − ∇
λ oil
∇ T
where ρ , λ , and C represent density, thermal conductivity, and specific heat capacity, respectively; r and φ represent distance in the radial direction and angle in the cylindrical-coordinate system, respectively; μ ,
T oil
, and p represents the dynamic viscosity, temperature, and pressure of mineral oil, respectively. m , h , and A represent the mass of the battery, the convective heat transfer coefficient, and the effective surface area, respectively.

To calibrate and validate the battery heat generation equation, a 3D single battery cell model was built in the Multiphysics simulation software, COMSOL. A battery module with BTMs using immersion cooling was selected to investigate the influence of battery aging on the thermal behaviour of battery modules. A PARDISO solver with an initial time step of 0.1 s is employed to computer state variables by partial differential equations. Fig. 4 shows the schematic of the module model and coolant inlet/outlet. Mineral oil was selected as the coolant of BTMs [33]. The external boundary conditions of the BTMs model are set as natural convection with a heat transfer coefficient of 10 W/m2/K [34,35]. Table 1 lists the key thermophysical properties of the battery cell and mineral oil. Three resistances (R
ohm = 25/50/75 mΩ) and various inlet coolant flow velocities (V
inlet = 0.01–0.12 m/s) were tested. Table 2 lists boundary conditions and simulation setup. A grid independence study is performed to choose an appropriate mesh size for the model to ensure simulation accuracy and save computational time. The average temperature of the battery module at a 1C discharge rate and the flow velocity of 0.12 m/s was utilized as the criteria for determining grid independence verification. Fig. 5 illustrates the grid independence test. The average temperature results of the battery module are stabilized when the grid number exceeds 3,882,806, with a minimum element size of 0.4 mm.

Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO) is an algorithm inspired by animal swarms. The optimization of the heat generation model is performed through co-simulations in MATLAB and COMSOL. Fig. 6 shows the flow chart of the application of PSO in optimizing heat generation for the LiB cell. Generally, the PSO mechanism consists of three main processes [38]: Firstly, each agent starts from a random initial position chosen within the search constraints. Each particle, in this paper, is an 8 × 1 matrix, representing 8 factors in f(soc). Each factor was given limits of [−0.9, 1.1] as boundary constraints. Using the initial position, the cost function of each agent is the sum of the square of the difference between all measured and predicted temperature points. The optimal position is the one that achieves the minimum cost function. Then, based on the current position and the optimal position of the agent, the position of each agent updates in each iteration. The movement of each agent was based on three elements: the current position, the best position, and a random movement. The global best of the particles in the search space is
P best
. The local best particle of the current population is
G best
. Each particle will calculate its objective function base on the position information, and the modified velocity and position of the particle can be expressed as follow:
(11)
v j
i + 1
= ω
v j i
+
c 1
θ 1 i
P best i
−
x j i
+
c 2
θ 2 i
G best i
−
x j i
(12)
x j
i + 1
=
x j i
+
v j
i + 1
where
x j i
and
v j i
denote the velocity and position of the particle, respectively;
c 1
and
c 2
denote two attraction factors;
θ 1 k
and
θ 2 k
denote random numbers between (0,1); ω , j , and i denote the weight factor, the index number of agents, and the number of iterations, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Calibration and validation for heat generation model
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 7 illustrated the results from the simulation and experiments before and after the optimization of the heat generation equation. Experiments of 0.5C–3.5C continuous discharge of 21,700 batteries were conducted and the data obtained were used to calibrate the heat generation equation. The reason that the maximum continuous discharge rate was limited to 3.5C is that the battery temperature rises too fast at this current rate, which might lead to potential safety risks. During these tests, the maximum temperature was set at 50 °C to avoid thermal runaway risks. As shown in Fig. 7a and c, the simulation results of the numerical model established by the unoptimized heat generation equation are significantly discrepant from the experimental data by up to 5 °C. Larger errors were observed for data from tests conducted at 1.5C and 2C. The differences in experimental and simulation results were derived from the calculation of the heat generation rate in simulation. There are several reasons for this discrepancy. The model provides results from mathematical questions and their boundary conditions, which do not fully describe the actual physical phenomena occurring in the reality. In addition, the internal resistance and voltage coefficient (dU/dT) in the battery heat generation equation are modified by introducing several factors, and these factors were optimized by the PSO algorithm. After the heat generation equation was optimized, the maximum discrepancy was reduced to 0.5 °C on average. Table 3 lists the values for factors in f(soc).

The next step was to validate the model to prove that the well-matched calibration data were not due to over-fitting optimization. A simple dynamic discharge load was applied to the batteries. The dynamic discharge load was chosen to represent a different discharge mode with the constant current discharge mode. Fig. 8 shows validation results from the simulation and experiments before and after the optimization of the heat generation equation. Similarly, the model shows good agreement with the experimental data, which is reflected in the maximum temperature prediction error of only 1.5 °C (5.2 %).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Temperature rising of the battery module
ce:section.ce:para:
The validated heat generation model was used to predict the thermal behaviour of a battery module with aged batteries, representing an application scenario of second-life LiBs. The thermal behaviour of a small battery module was simulated, where cells with various internal resistance (R
ohm = 25/50/75 mΩ). A R
ohm = 25 mΩ represents a pristine cell. Immersion cooling was selected as the thermal management strategy. The cells with higher R
ohm require an increase in the coolant flow velocity compared with the pristine cell. The battery module with R
ohm = 25 mΩ and V
inlet = 0.01 m/s was chosen as the baseline case. Fig. 9 shows the temperature evolution of the battery module. Only five selected cases were presented, including three different internal resistance (R
ohm = 25/50/75 mΩ) with V
inlet = 0.01 m/s and two aged internal resistance (R
ohm = 50/75 mΩ) with the flow velocity that led to matched temperature with the baseline case. Fig. 10 shows the average temperature of the battery module at various coolant velocities at the end of the simulation. With a flow velocity of 0.01 m/s, a cell with a higher R
ohm leads to a much higher temperature, especially at higher C rate. For example, at 1C, the R
ohm = 25 mΩ at the end of the simulation has an average temperature of 24.3 °C, compared to 29.2 °C for the cell with a R
ohm = 50 mΩ. When the V
inlet increased from 0.01 m/s to 0.05 m/s, the cell with R
ohm = 50 mΩ has the same temperature as the cell with the baseline case. Similarly, the V
inlet = 0.12 m/s is required for the cell with a R
ohm = 75 mΩ to match the baseline case. It should be noted that in Fig. 10 (c), temperature of more than 80 °C is unrealistic for normal battery operation since thermal runaway would potentially be triggered. However, for illustration purposes, the temperature results of cells over 80 °C are still plotted in the figure. Power consumption is considered in this study. Table 4 lists the pressure drop and power consumption of oil cooling. The heat generation of the battery module that has not been transferred by the cooling system is reflected in the temperature rise. With the increase of the flow velocity, the power consumption of the pump increases significantly. The ideal power consumption of the pump applied for the mineral oil can be expressed as follow:
P pump
= ∆ p
V ⃑
where the ∆ p denotes the pressure drop from the inlet and outlet of the coolant duct;
V ⃑
denotes the volume flow velocity of mineral oil.

The spatial structure of the battery module and the flow direction of the coolant cause temperature non-uniformity among cells in the battery module. The effect of coolant flow velocity on the maximum/minimum temperature of the battery module at a 2C discharge rate is shown in Fig. 11 , wherein the battery module consists of cells with R
ohm = 50 mΩ. When the V
inlet increased from 0.05 m/s to 0.12 m/s, the cell with maximum temperature in the module at the end of the simulation decreased from 49.2 °C to 34.0 °C. Fig. 12 plots the maximum temperature differential in the battery module with various flow velocities at a 3C discharge rate. The simulation terminates when the temperature of any cell in the battery module reaches the threshold (T
threshold ). The threshold temperatures were set at the maximum optimal temperature of 35 °C [39] and the upper safe limit of 60 °C [40], respectively. The cells with a higher R
ohm led to an increase in total heat generation, which further aggravates the temperature gradient in the battery module. At a higher charging/discharging rate, R
ohm = 50/75 mΩ with various flow rates could have different simulation duration to reach the same threshold temperature. A cell with a lower R
ohm will take more time to reach the threshold temperature. For example, at a 3C discharge rate and a threshold temperature of 60 °C, the maximum temperature differential of the case (R
ohm = 50 mΩ and V
inlet = 0.05 m/s) and the case (R
ohm = 75 mΩ and V
inlet = 0.05 m/s) reached 16.1 °C and 11.8 °C, respectively. The maximum temperature of the case (R
ohm = 50 mΩ and V
inlet = 0.12 m/s) can only reach 56.8 °C, resulting in a drop in temperature difference. With the flow velocity increasing from 0.01 m/s to 0.12 m/s, at 3C and threshold temperature of 60 °C, the maximum temperature differential between cells with R
ohm = 50 mΩ increased from 6.4 °C to 17.1 °C. The increase in flow velocity could further exacerbate the temperature gradient within the battery module due to the flow direction of the mineral oil.

In this study, an equation in the literature for calculating battery heat generation was modified by introducing a series of correction factors to the irreversible heat part. Temperature experiments of 21,700 batteries were conducted under continuous discharge rate (0.5C–3.5C) and dynamic load tests to calibrate and validate the heat generation model, respectively. The Particle Swarm Optimization algorithm written in MATLAB was coupled with numerical simulation in COMSOL to optimize correction factors as a function of the state of charge. Additionally, the optimized thermal model was applied to simulate the thermal behaviour of a battery module (16 aged cells) with immersion cooling, which assesses the influence of LiBs aging on the design parameters of BTMs. The simulation results of the battery model at a flow velocity of 0.01 m/s and internal resistance of 25 mΩ were chosen as the baseline. The conclusions drawn from the study can be summarized as follows:
1.
The proposed correction factors could optimize the heat generation model of the lithium-ion battery cell at various constant discharge rates. The average temperature error between simulation results and experimental results was reduced from 5 °C to 0.5 °C, compared to the unoptimized model.
2.
By implementing a dynamic current load test, the results of the optimized numerical model demonstrated reasonably good agreement with the experimental data, with an error of only 5.2 %. This study demonstrates a simple and effective method of improving battery thermal modeling, which will significantly help engineers and researchers save simulation time and computational costs.
3.
The numerical analysis results showed that aged cells with higher internal resistance require much higher coolant flow and pump power. At the discharge rate of 2C, to reach the baseline temperature, the mineral oil flow velocity corresponding to the battery cells with internal resistances of 50 mΩ and 75 mΩ should be increased to 0.05 m/s and 0.12 m/s, respectively.

In future research, the thermal behaviour of batteries with immersion cooling under various operation conditions will be further studied experimentally and theoretically. Optimization works of battery thermal management systems will be also conducted, including the coolant selection, flow direction, battery cell layout, etc.

Zeyu Sun: Methodology, Formal analysis, Software, Writing – original draft. Yue Guo: Resources, Data curation. Cheng Zhang: Investigation, Writing – review & editing. Quan Zhou: Visualization, Writing – review & editing. Hongming Xu: Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Chongming Wang: Supervision, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare the following financial interests/personal relationships which may be considered as potential competing interests:
Chongming Wang reports financial support was provided by Innovate UK.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work is supported by Innovate UK (https://gtr.ukri.org/pro under the project ‘CALIBRE - Custom Automotive Lithium-Ion Battery Recycling’ (Grant Reference Number: 104176)).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Trends in electric mobility
Glob. EV Outlook 2020 Technol. Rep

J. Energy Storage

Bhagat, “The design and impact of in-situ and operando thermal sensing for smart energy storage,” J. Energy Storage, vol. 22, no. November 2018, pp. 36–43, 2019, doi: 10.1016/j.est.2019.01.026.
